subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree in Business Administration with a focus on marketing
Undergraduate degree in Marketing from a recognized university
Completed coursework in consumer behavior, data analytics, and supply chain management
Participated in workshops on retail merchandising and sales optimization

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Job title: Purchasing Agent (Store Marketer)
Responsible for promoting products and increasing customer traffic in a retail store environment
Role includes reviewing sales data, planning in-store promotions, and optimizing product displays
Collaborates with team members to implement marketing and purchasing strategies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the retail industry, specifically within a consumer goods store
Focuses on brick-and-mortar retail operations rather than wholesale or farm products
Operates in a competitive, customer-facing environment

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a mid-sized retail store with approximately 50-100 employees
Company culture emphasizes teamwork and adaptability
Store serves a diverse urban/suburban customer base

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Retail business selling consumer goods such as apparel, electronics, or home products
Targets a broad market segment, catering to local walk-in customers
Competes with both local and chain retailers in the area

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a purchasing agent and marketer in the current role
Began current position shortly after completing Master's degree
Still considered early-career in the field

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds an entry-level to early-mid career position
Acts as an individual contributor with some influence over marketing decisions
Reports to a store manager or marketing supervisor

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Analyzes daily and weekly sales data to identify product trends
Designs and implements in-store displays and merchandising layouts
Plans and coordinates promotional campaigns and discounts
Monitors inventory levels and works with suppliers as needed
Collaborates with store staff to ensure marketing initiatives are executed effectively

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends roughly 35% of time analyzing sales and customer data
Devotes about 30% to designing and updating in-store displays
Allocates 20% to planning promotions and marketing strategies
Remaining 15% involves meetings, supplier communication, and administrative tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Has moderate autonomy in designing displays and recommending promotions
Final approval for major campaigns or large purchases rests with management
Empowered to suggest changes based on sales data analysis
Limited budgetary authority; works within guidelines set by store leadership

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Quickly interprets basic sales data and identifies underperforming products
Enjoys brainstorming new marketing ideas or display concepts
Finds it straightforward to coordinate simple in-store promotions
Comfortable interacting with team members to implement routine tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Advanced data analysis, such as predicting long-term sales trends, is challenging
Designing multi-channel marketing campaigns requires significant planning
Negotiating terms with new suppliers can be complex due to limited experience
Managing multiple promotions simultaneously while tracking effectiveness is demanding

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Manually compiling weekly sales reports is repetitive and time-consuming
Tracking inventory levels and flagging low-stock items could be automated
Routine updates to product pricing in the system are data-heavy and suitable for automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Finds designing creative in-store displays highly enjoyable
Enjoys analyzing customer trends to develop targeted promotions
Considers repetitive administrative tasks, like data entry, tedious
Least enjoys manual inventory checks and paperwork

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Data from different systems doesn’t always sync, causing delays in reporting
Manual reporting increases risk of human error
Lack of automated tools for inventory and sales tracking slows workflow
Limited real-time customer insights hinder rapid response to trends

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels more productive and creative when working on tasks that match personal strengths, like display design
Enjoyment is highest when learning new marketing strategies and seeing direct results
Productivity drops during highly repetitive or manual data tasks
Skill level increases with exposure to new tools and marketing techniques

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally open and curious about new technologies that can improve work efficiency
Sees tech adoption as necessary for career growth in retail marketing
Appreciates tools that have clear, immediate benefits

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Adapts quickly to new software if given adequate training
Occasionally needs time to adjust to major system changes
Comfortable experimenting with new apps, especially if they relate to marketing analytics

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate several hours a month to tech training
Prefers short, focused tutorials over lengthy courses
Prioritizes learning technology that directly improves day-to-day tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by the potential to save time and reduce manual work
High workloads sometimes limit the ability to participate in training
Less likely to engage with tech perceived as irrelevant to current responsibilities

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers who share positive experiences with new tools increase willingness to adopt
Management encouragement is a strong motivator for tech adoption
Team collaboration on tool rollouts makes the transition easier

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses POS (Point of Sale) system to track sales and inventory
Relies on Microsoft Excel for compiling and analyzing sales reports
Occasionally uses Canva or Adobe Spark to design promotional materials
Accesses store’s internal database for product and supplier information

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Smartphone or tablet for quick communication and on-the-go access to sales data
Physical planograms and display guides for in-store merchandising
Email and internal messaging platforms for team coordination

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Familiar with retail inventory management software (e.g., Lightspeed, Vend)
Understands key functions of CRM (Customer Relationship Management) systems
Aware of industry-standard marketing platforms like Mailchimp for email campaigns

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: POS system does not always integrate seamlessly with inventory management software
Occasional data export/import errors between Excel and store database
Manual re-entry of sales figures is sometimes required due to lack of integration

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic AI concepts such as machine learning and natural language processing
Has read introductory articles on how AI analyzes large data sets
Familiar with terms like 'predictive analytics' and 'chatbot'

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Occasionally uses ChatGPT to generate marketing copy or brainstorm ideas
Has tried Google Analytics’ AI-driven insights for customer trend analysis
Tested Jasper AI for creating social media content drafts

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Uses AI tools around 2-3 times per month, mainly for content generation and data insights
Relies on LLMs for specific tasks such as writing promotional emails or ad copy
Occasionally leverages AI to quickly summarize customer feedback

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used ChatGPT to generate creative taglines that boosted campaign response
Experienced off-target results when relying on AI for highly localized promotions
Learned that AI suggestions work best when paired with human context and oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: No formal AI training provided by the company yet
Peers occasionally share tips or best practices informally
Self-taught through online tutorials and experimentation

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration with AI tools to maintain control over outputs
Uses AI as a brainstorming partner rather than a fully autonomous agent
Finds value in iterative feedback loops with AI suggestions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Sees AI as an assistant or advisor for creative and data-driven tasks
Uses AI to augment—not replace—existing decision-making processes
Occasionally treats AI as a 'co-worker' for brainstorming sessions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Open to experimenting with new AI-driven processes, especially if they save time
Prefers to see clear evidence of AI benefits before fully adopting
Appreciates gradual integration of AI into existing workflows

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces like chatbots for idea generation and Q&A
Finds command-based interfaces efficient for structured, repetitive tasks
Values flexibility to switch between interface styles depending on the task

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many decisions, such as local promotion design, require in-depth understanding of the store’s customer base
Tacit knowledge of customer preferences guides display and product placement choices
AI outputs often need to be adapted for local context and store culture

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Moderate trust in AI for routine content generation and data analysis
Prefers to double-check AI outputs before implementing major changes
Relies on personal judgment for high-stakes or customer-facing decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive data tasks and basic content generation
Believes human oversight is essential for strategy and customer engagement
Supports hybrid workflows where AI handles groundwork and humans make final decisions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: High automation is acceptable for back-office processes with clear, objective rules
Feels threatened if AI were to automate creative or relationship-driven tasks
Wants the option to intervene or override automated outputs when needed

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances data analysis skills by automating basic processing
Sees creative and interpersonal skills becoming more valuable as AI adoption grows
Worries that basic reporting skills may become less critical over time

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing skills in prompt engineering for AI content generation
Learning to interpret and validate AI-generated insights for local application
Increasing responsibility for integrating AI tools into daily workflow

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mild concern about automation of repetitive reporting and data entry tasks
Does not feel immediate threat to overall role due to emphasis on creativity and customer understanding
Stays informed about AI developments to remain adaptable

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: Always reviews AI-generated content before public release
Manual data checks are standard practice for sales reporting
Prefers systems that allow human intervention at key decision points

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Company is beginning to explore AI tools but lacks formal policies
Team discussions about AI adoption have increased in the past year
No mandatory AI use yet, but management encourages experimentation

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as a major driver of change in retail and marketing over the next decade
Believes AI will create new job categories as well as automate existing tasks
Cautiously optimistic about AI’s potential to improve customer experience

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Believes transparency in AI decision-making is important for trust
Worries about potential for bias in AI-driven customer analytics
Supports responsible use of AI with clear human oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from AI tools that can analyze hyper-local customer sentiment in real time
Desires seamless integration between AI analytics and existing POS/inventory systems
Wants more intuitive AI interfaces for non-technical staff

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Expects more time will be spent on strategy and creative problem-solving
Predicts many manual reporting tasks will be fully automated
Believes the role will require ongoing learning and adaptation to new AI tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Introduce formal AI training sessions for staff at all levels
Pilot AI tools in non-critical workflows before full-scale rollout
Create feedback channels for staff to report issues and suggest AI improvements
Set clear guidelines on responsible and ethical AI use
